summary: Gruber examines how Mac OS X's system-wide 'Search in Google' contextual menu command always opens Safari, even when the user has set a different default browser. He notes that while Apple generally respects the user's default browser choice, this Cocoa text system feature is a notable exception. He argues that Apple could easily construct a Google search URL and open it in the default browser, but chooses not to because routing searches through Safari's toolbar preserves Google affiliate revenue for Apple. It's a small but telling example of Apple prioritizing revenue over user preference.
key_points: Mac OS X generally respects the user's default browser choice, but the system-wide 'Search in Google' contextual menu always opens Safari
The feature hooks into Safari's Google Search field rather than simply constructing a URL for the default browser
Apple's likely motivation is preserving Google affiliate search revenue that flows through Safari
Other Safari tie-ins like iTunes bookmark syncing are more defensible since they involve file format parsing
money_quotes: A simpler way to implement the feature would be to construct a Google search URL containing the terms and open that URL in the default browser.
This would make the feature work equally well in all browsers — but would deprive Apple of a few pennies in Google affiliate lucre for each such search.
It's the only tie-in to Safari that really irks me.
key_insight: Apple's 'Search in Google' contextual menu bypasses the user's default browser choice in favor of Safari, likely to preserve Google affiliate revenue — a small but telling prioritization of money over user experience.
